AI Usage
Governance
Policy and Standards for Responsible AI Deployment
A complete, self-sufficient governance policy for a cross-sector enterprise deploying AI tools across business functions. One policy from a 27-policy governance framework. No companion document required to understand and apply it.
Document IDLP-PORT-POL-AI_Usage_Governance-Enterprise-Screen-v1_0
Document OwnerChief Compliance Officer · AI Governance Lead
Applies ToAll employees, contractors, and vendors using AI tools on behalf of or within systems of the organization
Review FrequencyAnnual · or within 30 days of a material AI-related incident or regulatory change
Next Review DateMay 2027
Approval AuthorityChief Executive Officer · Board-level AI Governance Committee
Standards ReferencedEU AI Act (2024) · NIST AI RMF (2023) · ISO/IEC 42001 (2023) · India DPDP Act 2023

Section 1
01
Document Control
Every policy that governs others must itself be governed. This section establishes ownership, version authority, classification, and distribution. All amendments require formal approval before taking effect — and all approved changes must be communicated to every person in scope.
1.1Document Identification and Ownership
Internal — Restricted Distribution
Core Identification
Document Title
AI Usage Governance Policy: Standards for Responsible AI Deployment
Document ID
LP-PORT-POL-AI_Usage_Governance-Enterprise-Screen-v1_0

Version
1.0 — Initial Release

1.2Version History
VersionDateAuthorApproved ByNature of Change
1.0May 2026Chief Compliance OfficerCEO · Board AI CommitteeInitial release. Complete 18-section AI Usage Governance Policy governing all AI tool deployment across business functions. Includes five appendices: Approved Tools Register, Prohibited Use Examples, Risk Classification Matrix, Incident Report Form, and Exception Request Form.
Subsequent versions to be recorded here. All amendments require approval by the authorities named in Section 1.1 before the revised version is published and communicated.
1.3Distribution and Scope of Obligation

This policy applies to every individual listed below without exception. Receiving a controlled copy of this policy constitutes acknowledgement that the individual is aware of and bound by its provisions. Distribution is controlled — this policy must not be shared beyond the list below without written approval from the Policy Owner.

Role / GroupCopy TypeBasis of Obligation
All full-time employeesControlledEmployment contract + organization-wide policy obligation. Training (Section 14) is mandatory within 30 days of this policy's effective date.
All part-time employeesControlledEmployment contract. Obligation applies proportionally to AI tool use within the role.
Contractors and consultants (accessing organizational systems)ControlledService agreement inclusion. Contractors using organizational AI tools or accessing organizational data through AI tools are in scope per Section 3.2.
Vendors (providing AI tools or AI-enabled services)Controlled — referenceVendor agreement inclusion. Vendors whose tools are listed in Appendix A are subject to the obligations in that register.
Chief Executive OfficerControlledApproval authority and mandatory leadership commitment (ISO/IEC 42001 Clause 5.1 requirement). Board AI Governance Committee.
AI Governance Committee (Board level)Controlled — masterGovernance oversight. Approval authority for this policy and all material amendments.
Chief Compliance Officer (Policy Owner)Controlled — masterPolicy ownership, operational authority, and annual review responsibility.
AI Governance LeadControlled — masterDay-to-day operational custodian. Maintains Appendix A (Approved Tools Register) and chairs the AI Governance Review Group.
1.4Amendment Obligations

This policy must be reviewed and, if required, amended under any of the following conditions — regardless of whether the scheduled annual review date has been reached:

Mandatory Review Triggers
  • A material AI-related incident (as defined in Section 13) is reported — review Section 13 within 14 days and the full policy within 30 days.
  • A regulatory update to the EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 42001, or the India DPDP Act materially changes an obligation addressed by this policy — review affected sections within 60 days of the update taking effect.
  • A major AI model provider announces a capability or architectural change that materially alters the risk profile of any tool listed in Appendix A — review Appendix A and Section 11 within 30 days.
  • The organization adds a new business function that uses AI tools not covered by the current Appendix A — review and update Appendix A within 14 days of operational commencement.
  • The organization's headcount, geographic footprint, or client base changes in a way that alters applicable regulatory obligations — review Section 9 and Section 3 within 60 days.
  • An Exception Request (Appendix E) is granted for a use case that subsequently reveals a gap in Section 7 or Section 8 — review and update the relevant section within 30 days of the exception's operational commencement.
Section 2
02
Purpose & Rationale
AI without governance is not AI adoption. It is risk accumulation. This policy exists because the pace of AI deployment has outrun the organizational structures designed to manage its consequences.
2.1The Problem This Policy Addresses

Most organizations deploying AI tools in 2025 and 2026 are operating without formal governance. The research evidence is consistent: only 9% of organizations have a mature AI governance framework, 95% of executives have experienced at least one AI-related incident, and 23% of firms have no formal AI policy in place at all. The primary cause is not ignorance of the risk — it is the absence of a structure that makes responsible AI use the default, not the exception.

The gap this policy closes is specific. It is not a philosophical statement about AI ethics. It is an operational instrument — a set of rules, roles, processes, and accountability structures that determine who can use which AI tools, for what purposes, under what conditions, and with what obligations. Without it, AI use is person-dependent: inconsistent across functions, unauditable by design, and fragile when accountability is required.

This policy changes that. Its standard is self-sufficiency: the reader needs no other document, person, or system to understand what is permitted, what is prohibited, what to do when something goes wrong, and how to operate within the framework every day.

2.2The Regulatory Moment

This policy is written in May 2026. The regulatory environment for AI is no longer theoretical. The EU AI Act's prohibited practices have been enforceable since February 2025. Obligations for providers of General-Purpose AI models have applied since August 2025. High-risk AI system requirements under Annex III take full force in August 2026 — three months from this policy's issue date. Non-compliance carries penalties of up to €35 million or 7% of global annual turnover for the most serious violations.

For organizations with any operations or customers in the European Union, compliance is not optional. For organizations without EU operations, the NIST AI Risk Management Framework has become the de facto baseline in US markets and federal procurement, and ISO/IEC 42001 is increasingly a procurement requirement from enterprise clients in financial services, healthcare, and the public sector. The regulatory trajectory in every major market is the same: requirements will only increase in scope and stringency.

This policy is designed to satisfy the core obligations of all three — EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, and ISO/IEC 42001 — as a single, unified governance instrument, without duplicating work across separate compliance streams.

2.3What This Policy Does
This policy governs four things
  • Authorization: which AI tools may be used, by whom, for what purposes, and at what risk level — without requiring approval every time (Sections 7, 8, Appendix A).
  • Accountability: who owns which governance decisions, who must be consulted, and who is informed — across the full range of AI governance activities (Section 6).
  • Protection: what safeguards apply to data, intellectual property, and individuals affected by AI-assisted decisions — and what the organization must not do under any circumstances (Sections 8, 9, 10).
  • Recovery: what happens when something goes wrong — how incidents are reported, classified, investigated, and remediated — and how near-misses are captured before they become incidents (Section 13, Appendix D).

This policy does not restrict AI adoption. It structures it. The exception process (Section 15) and the four-tier use taxonomy (Section 7) are designed specifically to enable new and innovative AI uses within a governance framework — rather than suppressing them through blanket prohibition or ad hoc judgment.

2.4Relationship to the Process Quality Companion SOP

This policy is one instrument in a larger governance body. Its companion document — the Process Quality and Audit Preparation SOP (LP-PORT-SOP-ISO_CMMI_Audit_Prep-IT_Services) — governs the organizational processes, evidence management, and audit readiness required for ISO 9001:2015 and CMMI v3.0 compliance. Together, they form the documentation core of a governance system that treats AI and process quality as interlocking disciplines, not separate silos.

Specifically: this policy's incident reporting framework (Section 13) is aligned with the NCR and corrective action process in the companion SOP. This policy's compliance monitoring framework (Section 12) uses the same internal audit architecture as the companion SOP's Section 8. And this policy's review cycle (Section 16) uses the same mandatory trigger mechanism as the companion SOP's Section 1.4. The two documents are designed to be operated by the same governance team — not by separate functions working in parallel.

LP-PORT-POL-AI_Usage_Governance-Enterprise-Screen-v1_0 · Version 1.0 · Internal — Restricted Lucent Prism · nidhiagarwal@lucentprism.com
Section 3
03
Scope
This policy governs all AI tool use within, on behalf of, or in the systems of the organization. The scope is intentionally broad — the risks of ungoverned AI use do not diminish because a contractor rather than an employee made the decision.
3.1Who This Policy Covers
CategoryScope and Basis
All full-time and part-time employeesUniversally in scope. All AI tool use in a professional capacity — including personal devices when used for organizational work — is covered by this policy.
Contractors and consultants accessing organizational systemsIn scope for the duration of their engagement. Service agreements must include a provision requiring compliance with this policy as a condition of access. Responsibility for ensuring this provision is in the agreement rests with the contracting function.
Vendors providing AI tools or AI-enabled servicesIn scope for obligations specific to tool providers: data handling, DPA status, and the information they must provide for entry in Appendix A. Vendors whose tools are not in Appendix A may not be used for organizational work.
Interns and work placement individualsIn scope from Day 1. The supervising manager is responsible for ensuring that the mandatory foundation training (Section 14.1) is completed within the first five days of the engagement.
3.2What This Policy Covers — AI Tool Definition

This policy covers any software system that uses machine learning, generative AI, or language model technology to perform tasks, generate outputs, make or support decisions, or take actions. The following are in scope regardless of how they are described or marketed:

In Scope — AI Tool Categories
  • Dedicated generative AI tools (such as large language model chat interfaces, AI writing assistants, AI code generation tools, and AI image generation systems).
  • AI features embedded in business software platforms — including but not limited to AI writing features in productivity suites, AI-assisted search and summarization in knowledge management tools, AI recommendations in CRM systems, and AI-generated content in design tools.
  • AI-powered decision-support systems used for hiring, performance management, credit assessment, fraud detection, or any other function that affects individuals.
  • Custom or internally developed AI systems, whether built on third-party foundation models or developed independently.
  • Agentic AI systems — systems capable of taking autonomous actions (sending messages, executing transactions, accessing or modifying systems) without per-action human approval.
  • AI tools accessed through personal accounts (e.g., a personal subscription to an AI service) when used for organizational work or with organizational data.
3.3Scope Exclusions
Excluded From This Policy
  • Traditional algorithmic tools (rule-based systems, deterministic software, spreadsheet formulas) that do not use machine learning or generative AI components — unless they interface with or feed into a covered AI system.
  • AI tools used exclusively for personal purposes and with no organizational data — provided they are accessed through a personal device and personal account, and no organizational systems or credentials are involved.
  • Research activities conducted under a separately approved research ethics framework — where that framework explicitly addresses AI tool governance for the specific research context.
  • AI-powered consumer products used in a personal capacity (navigation, personal assistants, consumer entertainment) with no connection to organizational data or systems.

Doubt rule: If there is any doubt about whether a tool or use case is in scope, treat it as in scope and consult the AI Governance Lead before proceeding. The purpose of this rule is not to create administrative burden — it is to prevent the most common governance failure: ungoverned use rationalized after the fact.

3.4Geographic and Regulatory Reach

This policy applies to all organizational operations regardless of the jurisdiction in which work is performed or in which AI output is used. Where local laws impose obligations that exceed the requirements of this policy, those local obligations take precedence and must be flagged to the AI Governance Lead within 14 days of identification.

Organizations with operations or customers in the European Union are subject to the EU AI Act's provisions, which have been enforceable in stages since February 2025. For organizations with operations in India, the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 applies to all AI processing involving personal data of Indian data principals — including processing that occurs outside India. Section 9 addresses these obligations in detail.

Section 4
04
Definitions & Acronyms
Every term used operationally in this policy is defined here. Terms are written for comprehension — no prior knowledge of AI regulation or technology is assumed. If a term is used in this policy and not found here, report it to the AI Governance Lead as a gap.
4.1Acronym Register
AcronymFull FormOperational Meaning in This Policy
CCOChief Compliance OfficerThe Policy Owner. Holds operational authority for this policy, chairs the AI Governance Review Group, and reports to the CEO on AI governance matters.
DPAData Processing AgreementA contractual agreement between the organization and an AI tool provider specifying how personal data is processed, stored, and protected. Required for any tool in Appendix A that handles personal data.
DPDP ActDigital Personal Data Protection Act 2023India's data protection legislation governing the processing of personal data of Indian data principals. Applies to offshore operations involving Indian personal data.
EU AI ActEuropean Union Artificial Intelligence ActThe first comprehensive AI regulation in the EU, establishing a risk-based framework. Prohibited practices enforceable from February 2025; high-risk system requirements in full force August 2026.
GDPRGeneral Data Protection RegulationEU data protection regulation. Relevant to this policy where AI tools process personal data of EU/EEA residents, including offshore processing.
GPAIGeneral-Purpose AI ModelAn AI model that can perform a wide range of tasks and serve as the foundation for downstream AI applications. Examples include GPT-4, Claude, and Gemini. GPAI models have specific obligations under the EU AI Act as of August 2025.
ISO 42001ISO/IEC 42001:2023The first international standard for AI Management Systems. Certifiable. Provides structured requirements for establishing and maintaining responsible AI governance. This policy is designed to support ISO 42001 compliance.
NIST AI RMFNIST Artificial Intelligence Risk Management FrameworkVoluntary US framework for managing AI risks, structured around four functions: Govern, Map, Measure, Manage. De facto baseline in US markets and federal procurement.
RACIResponsible, Accountable, Consulted, InformedResponsibility framework used in Section 6. R = does the work; A = owns the outcome; C = input required; I = kept informed.
4.2Key Definitions
TermDefinition
Agentic AIAn AI system capable of taking autonomous actions — sending messages, executing transactions, accessing or modifying systems, or triggering workflows — without per-action human approval. Agentic AI is automatically classified as Category 2 Risk (Section 11) regardless of other factors, and requires explicit authorization under Section 7 (Tier 4) before deployment.
AI-Assisted DecisionAny decision made by a human where AI-generated analysis, recommendations, or outputs materially informed the outcome. AI-assisted decisions are subject to human accountability — the human who acted on the AI output is responsible for the decision, regardless of the quality of the AI's contribution.
AI-Generated OutputAny text, image, code, audio, video, analysis, or data produced primarily by an AI system rather than a human. AI-generated outputs require appropriate attribution, review, and oversight as specified in Sections 7 and 10.
AI Governance LeadThe AI Governance Lead is the operational custodian of this policy. This is a designated named role within the organization — not a committee and not a job title suffix. The AI Governance Lead maintains Appendix A, processes exception requests, coordinates the AI Governance Review Group, and is the first point of contact for all questions arising under this policy.
Approved AI ToolAny AI tool listed in Appendix A with a current approval status. Only Approved AI Tools may be used for organizational work. A tool is not approved by virtue of being commercially available, widely used, or recommended by a colleague — it must appear in Appendix A.
Data ClassificationThe organization's formal system for categorizing data by its sensitivity and the controls required to protect it. For the purposes of this policy, the relevant classifications are: Public, Internal, Confidential, and Restricted. Appendix A specifies the maximum data classification permitted for each approved tool.
HallucinationThe generation by an AI system of factually incorrect, fabricated, or internally inconsistent content presented as accurate. Hallucination is an inherent property of current generative AI systems, not a defect. Users are responsible for verifying AI-generated factual claims before acting on or communicating them.
Shadow AIThe use of AI tools that have not been approved under this policy, either because the user is unaware of the approval requirement or has chosen to proceed without it. Shadow AI is a prohibited use under Section 8 and a reportable incident under Section 13. The prohibition applies regardless of whether the shadow tool produces good results — ungoverned good outcomes are still ungoverned.
AI IncidentAny event in which an AI tool produces an output, takes an action, or contributes to a decision that causes or has the potential to cause harm — to individuals, to the organization, to clients, or to the integrity of organizational data. Includes but is not limited to: AI-generated discriminatory outputs, data exposure through AI interfaces, hallucination used in a consequential decision, unauthorized AI action in an agentic system, and regulatory non-compliance arising from AI use.
Near-MissAn event in which an AI-related harm was possible but did not occur — because it was caught before acting on the output, because a safeguard functioned as intended, or because of circumstances outside the organization's control. Near-misses are reportable under Section 13 and protected from adverse consequences under the same section's reporter protection provisions.
Training Data ProhibitionThe rule established in Section 9.4 of this policy: the organization may not use proprietary, client, or personal data to fine-tune or train any external AI model without explicit written consent from all data subjects and documented approval from the CCO and the relevant data controller.
Section 5
05
Policy Principles
Seven principles govern this policy. They are not aspirations — they are the governing logic from which every rule in every subsequent section is derived. When a situation arises that this policy does not explicitly address, these principles are the decision framework. When a rule in this policy seems to conflict with good judgment, return to the principles.
I Human Accountability Is Non-Negotiable
AI systems do not carry accountability. The human who deploys, directs, or acts on an AI system does.
Behavioral implication: No AI output — regardless of its quality, source, or apparent confidence — transfers decision-making responsibility away from the human who uses it. Using AI to support a decision does not reduce accountability for that decision. Delegating a task to an AI system does not delegate the obligation to own the outcome. This applies regardless of whether the AI performed well or poorly.
II Data Does Not Leave the Perimeter Without Authorization
Organizational, client, and personal data shared with an AI tool is shared with the tool's provider. Act accordingly.
Behavioral implication: Before entering any data into an AI tool, ask: is this data authorized for the data classification level permitted for this tool (Appendix A)? If the answer is no, or if you don't know, stop. The convenience of an AI output never justifies unauthorized data exposure. This includes summarizing confidential documents, pasting client data into prompts, and attaching internal files to AI interfaces.
III Explainability Scales With Risk
The higher the risk of an AI-assisted decision, the greater the organization's obligation to explain how that decision was reached.
Behavioral implication: Category 1 and Category 2 AI uses (Section 11) require documented rationale — not just the AI output, but the human reasoning applied to it. If a decision affecting an individual's employment, credit, health, or legal status is challenged, "the AI said so" is not a defense. The documentation of human judgment is the defense. Risk category determines documentation obligation; the AI Governance Lead maintains the standard per category.
IV AI Literacy Is a Job Requirement
Using an AI tool without completing the required training for that tool and use case is not permitted — regardless of how familiar the tool appears.
Behavioral implication: The training requirements in Section 14 are not optional professional development. They are prerequisites for tool access. The AI Governance Lead maintains access controls aligned with training completion records. Familiarity with a consumer version of an AI tool does not satisfy the organizational training requirement — the organizational training addresses data classification, policy obligations, and function-specific risks that consumer familiarity does not cover.
V IP Ownership Is Asserted Before Use
The organization's intellectual property position on AI-generated outputs must be determined before those outputs are used, published, or submitted — not after.
Behavioral implication: Section 10 defines three IP ownership scenarios. The scenario that applies to a given output must be identified before that output is used consequentially — not when a copyright challenge or IP dispute arises. For Tier 3 and Tier 4 uses, the IP assessment is documented as part of the use authorization process. For Tier 1 and Tier 2 uses, the user is responsible for understanding which scenario applies and acting accordingly.
VI Continuous Monitoring Is the Default
AI governance that depends on periodic review is not governance. It is retrospective damage assessment.
Behavioral implication: The monitoring framework in Section 12 is designed for continuous operation, not annual audit. The four KPIs are measured monthly. Incidents and near-misses are reported as they occur. The Approved Tools Register (Appendix A) is maintained as a live document — not updated at the annual review. When an AI tool's risk profile changes (because the provider has changed its model, its data handling practices, or its capabilities), the register is updated within 30 days, not at the next review cycle.
VII Autonomous Action Requires Delegated Authority
An AI system that can act without human approval for each individual action must have explicit, documented authority — granted in advance — for every category of action it is permitted to take.
Behavioral implication: Agentic AI systems — systems capable of taking autonomous actions such as sending emails, executing transactions, or triggering workflows — may not be deployed or activated without explicit Tier 4 authorization (Section 7). The authorization must specify: what actions the system is permitted to take; what data it may access; what human oversight mechanism triggers review; and what conditions cause the system to pause and request human intervention. An agentic AI operating within its documented authority is permitted. An agentic AI operating beyond its documented authority — regardless of how beneficial the action appears — is a reportable incident under Section 13.

These seven principles are the floor of this policy, not the ceiling. They govern the situations this policy explicitly addresses. They also govern the situations it does not — because governance must be able to reason about the future, not only codify the past.

Section 6
06
Roles & Responsibilities
AI governance fails most often not because the rules are wrong but because ownership is unclear. Eight roles govern this policy. Each has a defined authority, a defined obligation, and a defined relationship to every other role. Ambiguity between them is not acceptable.
6.1Role Profiles
RoleReporting ToResponsibilities Under This Policy
Chief Compliance Officer
Policy Owner		CEOOwns this policy. Approves amendments. Chairs the AI Governance Review Group (quarterly). Reports AI governance performance to the CEO and Board AI Governance Committee. Has authority to suspend any AI tool from Appendix A if an unacceptable risk is identified — without waiting for a formal review cycle.
AI Governance Lead
Operational Custodian		CCODay-to-day custodian of this policy. Maintains Appendix A (Approved Tools Register) as a live document. Processes exception requests within 10 business days. Coordinates incident investigation (Section 13). Delivers mandatory training programme (Section 14). First point of contact for all policy questions. Reports monthly to CCO on all four KPIs (Section 12).
Board AI Governance CommitteeBoardGovernance oversight body. Approves this policy and material amendments. Receives quarterly AI governance reports from the CCO. Sets the organization's risk appetite for AI. Has authority to call for an emergency policy review at any time.
Chief Executive OfficerBoardApproves this policy with the Board AI Governance Committee. Demonstrates visible leadership commitment to responsible AI — a specific ISO/IEC 42001 requirement verified through leadership review. Receives escalations under Section 13 for Category 1 incidents. Approves exception requests involving Category 2 Risk or above (Section 15).
Department Heads / Function LeadsCEOResponsible for ensuring that all staff in their function have completed mandatory training (Section 14) within the required timeframe. Accountable for AI tool use within their function complying with this policy. Approve Tier 3 use requests (Section 7) within their function. Escalate Tier 4 use requests and any function-level AI governance concerns to the AI Governance Lead.
IT and Security FunctionCTO / CISOResponsible for technical controls governing AI tool access (authentication, data classification enforcement, logging). Manages DPA execution with tool providers. Notifies the AI Governance Lead within 24 hours of any security event involving an AI tool. Advises on risk classification for new tool submissions.
Legal and Compliance FunctionCCO / General CounselAdvises on regulatory obligations (EU AI Act, GDPR, DPDP Act) as they apply to specific AI uses. Reviews and approves DPAs. Advises on IP ownership determinations (Section 10) for Tier 3 and Tier 4 uses. Conducts legal review of new tool submissions where AI Act compliance is material.
All Employees and ContractorsRespective managersFollow this policy. Complete mandatory training within required timeframes. Use only Approved AI Tools (Appendix A) for organizational work. Report incidents and near-misses within 24 hours of identification (Section 13). Cooperate with any audit or investigation arising under this policy.
ISO/IEC 42001 requires a designated AI management representative. This role maps to the AI Governance Lead. The designation must be documented in writing by the CEO and reviewed annually.
6.2RACI Matrix — 12 AI Governance Activities

Key: A = Accountable (one per activity) · R = Responsible (does the work) · C = Consulted · I = Informed · — = No formal obligation

Governance Activity CCO AI Gov Lead Board Committee CEO Dept Heads IT/Security Legal All Staff
1. Policy ownership and amendmentARCCICCI
2. Approved Tools Register maintenanceCA/RI—CRRI
3. Tier 3 use case authorizationCA——RCCI
4. Tier 4 / agentic AI authorizationARIRCRRI
5. Exception request processingAR—CCCCI
6. Risk classification of AI usesCA——CRRI
7. Mandatory training deliveryCA/RIIRIII
8. Incident reporting and triageCAIIIRCR
9. Category 1 incident escalation and responseRAIRCRRI
10. KPI measurement and reportingCA/RIIIC——
11. Annual policy review and governance reportARRCCCCI
12. DPA negotiation and executionCC———RA—
Section 7
07
Permitted AI Use
Permitted AI use is structured across four tiers. The tier determines what authorization is required before use — not whether use is permitted in principle. Every tier permits the use it governs; the difference is the level of oversight commensurate with the risk.

All permitted AI use — at every tier — is conditional on the following baseline requirements: (1) the tool must appear in Appendix A with a current approval status; (2) the user must have completed the mandatory training for their role (Section 14); and (3) the data used with the tool must not exceed the maximum data classification permitted for that tool in Appendix A. These are not tier-specific conditions — they apply to all use, at all tiers, at all times.

Tier 1 Pre-Approved Use — No Additional Authorization Required Low risk · Internal or Public data only

Tier 1 uses may be undertaken by any trained staff member with any tool listed as Tier 1 in Appendix A, without requesting separate authorization. The authorization is built into the tool approval process.

Permitted use categories: Grammar, spelling, and style review of documents containing no confidential or restricted data. Summarization and comprehension support for publicly available information. AI-assisted search, question-answering, and knowledge retrieval where no personal or confidential data is entered. Code completion and code review assistance where the code does not contain credentials, keys, or proprietary algorithms. Translation and language assistance for non-sensitive content.

Tier 1 obligations: The user is responsible for reviewing all AI output before use. AI-generated factual claims must be verified before relying on them or communicating them. Outputs must not be represented as original human work in contexts where that distinction is material — for example, in academic submissions, in client deliverables specified as human-authored, or in regulatory filings.

Tier 2 Approved With Standard Conditions — Manager Awareness Required Low-Moderate risk · Internal data permitted with conditions

Tier 2 uses are approved for tools listed as Tier 2 in Appendix A, with the conditions specified in this section. The user's direct manager must be aware that the use is occurring — not that they must approve each instance, but that they understand it is part of the workflow.

Permitted use categories: Drafting of internal communications, reports, and documents using internal (non-confidential, non-restricted) data — subject to human review and revision before distribution. Analysis, synthesis, and summarization of internal data where no personal data or restricted commercial information is involved. Research assistance using publicly available sources, with all AI-sourced claims verified before inclusion in any organizational output. Market research, competitive intelligence, and industry analysis synthesis — with factual claims verified. Transcription and summarization of meetings, calls, or recorded content where participants have been notified that AI transcription is in use.

Tier 2 obligations: All Tier 2 outputs must be reviewed and materially shaped by a human before distribution or use in decisions. The reviewer is accountable for the output as if they had authored it. Where Tier 2 AI output is included in a client deliverable, the engagement agreement must not prohibit AI assistance or must be updated before the output is submitted.

Tier 3 Approved Subject to Formal Use Case Review — Department Head Authorization Moderate-High risk · Confidential data may be involved · IP review required

Tier 3 uses require explicit written authorization from the relevant Department Head and confirmation from the AI Governance Lead that the data classification and risk level are compatible with the tool and use case. Authorization is granted per use case, not per user — once a use case is authorized, all users in that function may undertake it.

Use cases requiring Tier 3 authorization: AI tools used to draft, analyze, or summarize confidential client deliverables, proposals, or engagement materials. AI-assisted legal document drafting, contract review, or regulatory analysis. AI-assisted financial modeling, analysis, or forecasting where the outputs inform external-facing financial communications. Customer-facing AI chatbots or automated response tools. AI-assisted recruitment screening, candidate assessment, or performance evaluation. AI tools processing confidential or restricted organizational data at scale. AI tools used in any function where the output is directly communicated to clients, regulators, or the public without further editorial review.

Tier 3 authorization requirements: Written request submitted to the AI Governance Lead using the standard template. IP assessment completed per Section 10. Risk classification assessment per Section 11. Department Head signature. AI Governance Lead confirmation within 10 business days. Authorization recorded in Appendix A against the relevant tool entry.

Tier 4 Case-by-Case Authorization — CCO and CEO Approval Required High risk · Restricted data or autonomous action · All agentic AI · Board notification

Tier 4 uses require CCO and CEO approval before any operational deployment. All agentic AI systems are automatically Tier 4 regardless of the function or data involved. Tier 4 authorization is granted per system and per deployment context — a change in the system's capabilities, data access, or operational scope requires a new authorization.

Use cases requiring Tier 4 authorization: All agentic AI systems — systems capable of taking autonomous actions without per-action human approval (see Principle VII and the Agentic AI definition in Section 4). AI systems used to make, or to directly and materially inform, decisions affecting individuals' employment, compensation, promotion, or termination. AI systems used to make or inform decisions affecting individuals' access to credit, insurance, or financial services. AI systems processing restricted personal health data or sensitive personal information at scale. AI systems used in any externally regulated function where regulatory approval for AI use may be required. AI-generated content published externally without clear human editorial oversight.

Tier 4 authorization requirements: Formal risk assessment per Section 11. Legal review per Section 6 (Legal and Compliance role). DPA confirmed for all data the system will access. Full system specification documenting: permitted actions, data access scope, human oversight mechanism, escalation trigger, and termination conditions. CCO and CEO written approval. Board AI Governance Committee notification within 14 days of approval. Authorization reviewed every 90 days from operational commencement.

When the Tier Is Not Clear

If a use case does not clearly fit a single tier, apply the higher tier. The AI Governance Lead is available to clarify. A use case assessed at Tier 3 that subsequent review determines was Tier 2 is not a compliance issue — it is evidence of a governance culture that errs toward caution. The reverse is an incident.

Section 8
08
Prohibited AI Use
Seven uses are prohibited absolutely. No exception request, no business justification, no senior approval, and no technical workaround overrides them. The rationale for each prohibition is stated — not as invitation to debate, but because a rule without a rationale is fragile.
#ProhibitionWhy This Is ProhibitedRegulatory Basis
1 Using AI for social scoring — evaluating individuals based on behavior, social relationships, or predicted future behavior in ways unrelated to a specific, legitimate use case Social scoring systems cause harms that are structurally difficult to challenge or reverse. The individual being scored typically cannot see the inputs, question the model, or contest the outcome through transparent means. EU AI Act — Unacceptable Risk category (Article 5). Prohibited without exception since February 2025.
2 Using AI to manipulate individuals through subliminal techniques or by exploiting vulnerabilities — including age, disability, or cognitive state — to alter behavior in ways harmful to the individual The deliberate use of AI to manipulate rather than inform is incompatible with the organization's obligations to individuals and with the principle of human accountability. No legitimate business objective justifies it. EU AI Act — Unacceptable Risk category (Article 5). Prohibited without exception since February 2025.
3 Feeding confidential client data, restricted personal data, or proprietary organizational data into any AI tool not listed in Appendix A as authorized for that data classification Data shared with an AI tool is, in effect, data shared with the tool's provider and potentially used to train that provider's models. The harm from a data governance failure of this kind is typically irreversible. GDPR Article 28 (processor obligations). DPDP Act 2023 (Data Fiduciary obligations). Organizational data classification policy.
4 Using AI to generate content — text, audio, video, or image — that is designed to deceive recipients about its origin or nature, including undisclosed deepfakes, impersonation of identifiable individuals, or fabricated evidence Deception enabled by AI technology causes harms to individuals and to institutional trust that the organization cannot control once the content is in circulation. The reputational and legal exposure is severe and largely uninsurable. EU AI Act (certain biometric manipulation uses). Emerging domestic legislation in multiple jurisdictions on synthetic media. General tort liability for fraud and misrepresentation.
5 Using AI to discriminate against individuals based on protected characteristics — including race, gender, age, disability, religion, national origin, or sexual orientation — in decisions related to employment, credit, housing, or public services Discrimination through AI is discrimination. The fact that an AI system rather than a human made the discriminatory recommendation does not reduce the organization's liability or the harm to the individual. This prohibition covers both direct discrimination and indirect discrimination through proxies. Applicable domestic anti-discrimination law. EU AI Act (High-Risk AI — biometric categorization, employment, essential services). GDPR Article 22 (automated decision-making affecting individuals).
6 Using AI tools to circumvent, probe, or exploit the security controls, access restrictions, or privacy settings of any system — belonging to the organization, to clients, or to any third party The use of AI to attack or probe systems — including the organization's own systems, in the name of security testing — requires explicit security team authorization and a defined testing protocol. Unauthorized AI-assisted probing is indistinguishable from malicious activity and will be treated as such. Computer fraud and unauthorized access legislation in all applicable jurisdictions. Organizational information security policy.
7 Shadow AI use — deploying, accessing, or using any AI tool not listed in Appendix A for organizational work, regardless of the tool's commercial availability, perceived safety, or the quality of its output A shadow AI tool that produces good output is still an ungoverned tool: its data handling is unverified, its DPA is absent, its risk classification has not been assessed, and its use cannot be audited. The governance failure is structural, not outcome-dependent. Organizational policy (this document). ISO/IEC 42001 requirements for documented AI system inventory. Applicable data protection law where personal data is involved.
Consequences of Prohibited Use
  • Any confirmed prohibited use is an AI incident under Section 13 and triggers the incident response process. The severity classification depends on the prohibition category and the specific circumstances.
  • Prohibited use by an employee may constitute gross misconduct under the applicable employment terms, depending on the nature and consequences of the breach.
  • Prohibited use by a contractor constitutes a material breach of the service agreement and may result in immediate termination of the engagement.
  • The organization reserves the right to report prohibited uses to relevant regulators where applicable law requires or where disclosure is in the public interest.
Section 9
09
Data Governance & Privacy
Data shared with an AI tool has left the organization's control. This section governs what data may be shared, with which tools, under what conditions, and what the organization may never permit.
9.1Data Classification in the AI Context

The organization's standard data classification applies to AI tool use without modification. Every entry in Appendix A specifies the maximum data classification permitted for that tool. Users are responsible for knowing the classification of the data they intend to use and confirming that the tool is authorized for that classification before proceeding.

ClassificationDefinitionAI Tool Use Permitted
PublicInformation already in the public domain or intended for public releaseAny tool in Appendix A, without conditions beyond Tier requirements
InternalInformation intended for use within the organization, not for external distributionTools in Appendix A authorized for Internal classification. Tier 1 and Tier 2 baseline.
ConfidentialInformation with commercial sensitivity, client data, personal data governed by GDPR or DPDP Act, or information subject to professional secrecy obligationsOnly tools in Appendix A with explicit Confidential authorization and an active DPA. Tier 3 minimum.
RestrictedInformation whose unauthorized disclosure would cause severe harm — including personal health data, legal privilege communications, regulated financial data, and national security-adjacent informationOnly with explicit Tier 4 authorization. No general tools. Bespoke controls required per use case.
9.2Data Processing Agreements

A Data Processing Agreement (DPA) is mandatory for any AI tool that processes personal data or confidential organizational data. The IT and Security function, in collaboration with Legal, is responsible for executing DPAs before a tool is listed in Appendix A as authorized for Confidential or above data classification. The absence of a DPA for a tool that handles personal data is a compliance gap reportable under Section 13.

DPA Minimum Requirements
  • Clear specification of which categories of personal data the provider may process on the organization's behalf.
  • Explicit prohibition on using organizational data to train the provider's AI models without separate written consent.
  • Data retention and deletion provisions: the organization's data must be deletable on request and must not be retained beyond the purpose for which it was shared.
  • Security standards the provider must maintain, including breach notification obligations and timelines.
  • Sub-processor controls: the provider must notify the organization before engaging sub-processors that will process organizational data.
  • Cross-border transfer safeguards where data may be processed outside the originating jurisdiction.
9.3Regulatory Frameworks

GDPR (EU/EEA personal data): Where AI tools process personal data of individuals in the EU or EEA, GDPR obligations apply regardless of where the processing occurs. The organization's role as data controller means it is responsible for ensuring AI tool providers comply with GDPR as data processors. GDPR Article 22 imposes specific obligations on decisions made solely by automated means — any AI system that makes decisions affecting individuals without meaningful human review must be assessed against Article 22 before deployment.

India DPDP Act 2023: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 applies to the processing of personal data of Indian data principals, including where processing occurs outside India. The organization's obligations as a Data Fiduciary include: having a Data Protection Officer or equivalent designated role, maintaining a lawful basis for all processing, ensuring that AI tools processing Indian personal data are covered by appropriate contractual controls, and providing data principals with the ability to raise concerns about automated decisions.

9.4AI Training Data Prohibition
Absolute Prohibition — Training Data

The organization may not use proprietary data, client data, or personal data to fine-tune, train, or develop any external AI model without all of the following: (1) explicit written consent from the data controller or all identifiable data subjects as applicable; (2) a signed agreement with the AI model provider specifying the scope and conditions of training data use; and (3) written approval from the Chief Compliance Officer. This prohibition applies regardless of the perceived benefit to the organization or the apparent willingness of the AI provider to use the data for this purpose. The organization's data is the organization's data — not a contribution to the AI industry's training corpus.

This prohibition exists because the use of organizational or client data to train external models raises intellectual property, confidentiality, and data protection issues that are difficult or impossible to reverse once the training has occurred. The model retains traces of the training data in ways that cannot be reliably identified or extracted after the fact.

Section 10
10
Intellectual Property & Output Ownership
Who owns what an AI creates is one of the least settled questions in current law. This policy takes a position — not because the law is settled, but because the organization must act consistently regardless of the legal uncertainty.
10.1Three Ownership Scenarios

The organization applies one of three ownership scenarios to every AI output that is used consequentially — in a client deliverable, a public communication, an internal decision, or any context where IP ownership may subsequently matter. The applicable scenario must be identified before the output is used, not after a challenge arises.

ScenarioDescriptionIP StatusDocumentation Required
Scenario A
Human-authored with AI assistance		 A human author uses AI tools to support their work — for drafting assistance, research, editing, or other support functions — but the creative and intellectual substance of the output is the human's. The AI contribution is instrumental, not generative. IP retained by the organization or individual as applicable. Standard IP ownership applies. None required for Tier 1 and Tier 2 uses. For Tier 3 and Tier 4 uses: note in use authorization record that Scenario A applies and that the human contribution is primary.
Scenario B
Significant AI generation with substantial human direction		 The AI generates a substantial portion of the output — the structure, the language, the substance — but the human directed the generation with specific, detailed prompts and made meaningful editorial decisions about the result. The output is neither purely AI-generated nor primarily human-authored. IP may be claimed by the organization subject to disclosure requirements in applicable jurisdictions. Legal review required before submitting as original work in any regulated or contractually governed context. Prompt log or equivalent record of human direction. AI tool and model version used. Statement of human editorial decisions applied. For client deliverables: confirm the engagement agreement does not prohibit AI generation.
Scenario C
Fully AI-generated		 The output is generated by an AI with minimal or no substantive human direction beyond the prompt. The human contribution is the act of prompting and selecting the output — not the creation of its substance. IP not owned by the organization in most current legal frameworks. Cannot be submitted as original human work without disclosure. Cannot be registered for copyright in most jurisdictions without human authorship claim. Legal function must advise before any commercial use. Classification as Scenario C must be recorded. Legal review required before any commercial use, licensing, or external submission. Disclosure of AI generation required in any context where it is material to the recipient.
10.2Model Training Data Liability

AI models may have been trained on data that includes third-party copyrighted content. The legal status of AI outputs that reproduce or substantially replicate copyrighted training data is actively contested in courts across multiple jurisdictions. This creates a risk that AI-generated outputs — even those produced without deliberate intent to reproduce protected material — may infringe third-party copyright.

Users generating AI outputs for commercial purposes, for client deliverables, or for publication must take reasonable steps to verify that the output does not reproduce protected third-party material. Where substantial similarities to existing protected works are identified, the Legal function must be consulted before the output is used. This obligation is particularly acute for code generation (where the model may reproduce licensed code under different terms), for creative content (where the model may reproduce copyrighted text or visual elements), and for technical documentation (where the model may reproduce proprietary specifications or standards).

Risk · Compliance · Monitoring
The Governance Middle
Section 11
11
Risk Assessment & Classification
Every AI use the organization undertakes carries a risk profile. This section provides the framework for assessing, classifying, and controlling that risk. Risk classification is not a bureaucratic exercise — it determines the controls applied and the oversight required.
11.1Risk Classification Framework

The organization classifies AI risks across four categories, aligned with the EU AI Act's four-tier risk structure and adapted for internal governance use. The classification of an AI system or use case determines which controls apply — not as a suggestion but as a mandatory minimum. A system may be classified at a higher category than the framework minimum if the AI Governance Lead determines that the specific deployment context warrants it.

CategoryDescriptionExamplesMandatory Controls
Category 1
Critical		 AI systems that make or directly and materially inform decisions affecting individuals' fundamental rights, employment, credit, insurance, health, or access to essential services. Equivalent to EU AI Act High-Risk AI (Annex III). AI-assisted hiring and firing decisions. AI credit scoring. AI-driven health diagnostic support. AI systems used in law enforcement or judicial processes. Biometric identification systems. Tier 4 authorization mandatory. Board AI Committee notification. Human oversight with documented review of every decision. Explainability documentation for each decision. External legal review. Regulatory conformity assessment where required. 90-day operational review.
Category 2
High		 AI systems processing personal data at scale, customer-facing AI agents interacting on behalf of the organization, agentic AI systems, and AI systems used in regulated functions where errors could cause material harm. Customer-facing AI chatbots. Agentic AI (automatic Category 2 minimum). AI-assisted financial modeling for external communications. AI systems processing health-adjacent data. AI used in regulatory filing processes. Tier 3 or Tier 4 authorization. DPA mandatory. Risk assessment documented and approved by CCO. Human review mechanism defined. Incident escalation path specified. Quarterly operational review. All agentic AI systems are automatic Category 2 regardless of other factors.
Category 3
Moderate		 AI systems used for internal business analysis and decision support involving confidential data, AI-assisted content generation for external publication, and AI systems with significant operational impact but no direct individual rights implications. AI-assisted market research and analysis. AI drafting tools for client communications. AI code review in production environments. AI used for internal operational forecasting. AI sentiment analysis on client feedback. Tier 2 or Tier 3 authorization depending on data classification. Human review of all consequential outputs. Data classification controls enforced. Annual review of tool risk profile. Incident reporting per Section 13.
Category 4
Low		 AI systems used for general productivity support involving Public or Internal data, where no individual rights are implicated and errors produce rework rather than harm. AI writing and grammar assistance. AI-assisted search and summarization. AI code completion for non-production code. AI translation of internal non-sensitive content. Tier 1 or Tier 2 authorization. Mandatory foundation training completed. Data classification not to exceed Internal. Standard incident reporting if something goes wrong.
11.2Risk Scoring Matrix — Likelihood × Impact

For uses at Category 2 and above, a formal risk score is calculated using the likelihood × impact matrix below. The score is not the classification — the category in Section 11.1 is the classification. The score is a decision-support tool for determining whether additional controls are warranted within a category.

Likelihood →
Impact ↓		 Unlikely (1) Possible (2) Probable (3) Near-Certain (4)
Severe (4)4 — High8 — Critical12 — Critical16 — Critical
Major (3)3 — Moderate6 — High9 — Critical12 — Critical
Moderate (2)2 — Low4 — Moderate6 — High8 — High
Minor (1)1 — Low2 — Low3 — Moderate4 — Moderate
Risk score thresholds: 1–3 = Low (Category 4 controls apply). 4–5 = Moderate (Category 3 controls minimum). 6–8 = High (Category 2 controls minimum). 9–16 = Critical (Category 1 controls minimum, CCO review mandatory before deployment).
11.3Agentic AI Override
Override Rule — Agentic AI

Any AI system capable of taking autonomous actions without per-action human approval is automatically classified as Category 2 Risk, regardless of what the likelihood × impact matrix produces for that system's other risk factors. This override cannot be waived or reduced by any authorization process. It exists because the risk profile of autonomous action is structurally different from the risk profile of AI-assisted human decision-making — and because the governance controls for agentic AI (Tier 4 authorization, documented authority scope, defined oversight mechanisms, and termination conditions) are prerequisites for deployment, not optional enhancements.

11.4Classification Review Triggers

A tool or use case must be re-classified if any of the following occurs: the AI model underlying the tool is updated in a way that materially changes its capabilities; the data scope expands to include a higher classification level; the function moves from decision-support to autonomous action; a regulatory change adds the tool's use case to a higher-risk category; or an incident under Section 13 reveals a risk profile inconsistent with the current classification. Re-classification is initiated by the AI Governance Lead and confirmed by the CCO within 14 days of the triggering event.

Section 12
12
Compliance & Monitoring
Governance that is not measured is an aspiration. Four KPIs govern this policy's operational effectiveness. They are measured monthly, reported quarterly, and acted on when they deviate from target — not when the annual review arrives.
12.1Four Governance KPIs
KPI 1 — Policy Compliance Rate (PCR)

Formula: (Staff with current mandatory training ÷ Total in-scope staff) × 100. Target: ≥ 95%. Escalation: Below 85% triggers AI Governance Lead report to CCO within 5 business days and a recovery plan within 14 days. Owner: AI Governance Lead. Data source: Training completion records (Section 14).

KPI 2 — Approved Tools Rate (ATR)

Formula: (AI tool uses through Appendix A tools ÷ Total AI tool uses identified) × 100. Target: 100%. Any Shadow AI use identified reduces this ratio. Escalation: Any confirmed Shadow AI use triggers an incident report under Section 13 within 24 hours. Owner: IT and Security Function. Data source: IT access logs, incident reports, self-reporting.

KPI 3 — Incident Rate by Severity (IRS)

Definition: Number of AI incidents per 1,000 AI tool uses, segmented by severity tier (Critical, Major, Minor, Near-Miss). Target: No specific numerical target — trend monitoring. A rising trend in any severity tier requires root cause analysis within 30 days. Escalation: Any Category 1 incident triggers CCO and CEO notification within 24 hours. Owner: AI Governance Lead. Data source: Incident reports (Section 13, Appendix D).

KPI 4 — Exception Closure Rate (ECR)

Formula: (Exceptions closed or renewed by sunset date ÷ Total exceptions with elapsed sunset date) × 100. Target: ≥ 85% closed or renewed by sunset date. Exceptions not renewed on time are automatically suspended. Escalation: Any exception operating beyond its sunset date without renewal triggers an incident report. Owner: AI Governance Lead. Data source: Exception register (Appendix E).

12.2Audit and Governance Review Cadence
Review TypeFrequencyChairParticipantsRequired Output
Monthly AI Governance ReportMonthlyAI Governance LeadCCOAll four KPIs · Shadow AI instances identified · exceptions expiring within 30 days · incidents open or closed · Appendix A changes · filed within 5 business days of month end
Quarterly AI Governance ReviewQuarterlyCCOCEO · Department Heads · IT · LegalAggregated KPI performance · risk classification changes · material incidents and responses · policy gaps identified · Appendix A review · actions with named owners
Board AI Governance ReportQuarterlyBoard AI CommitteeCCO · CEORisk posture assessment · regulatory developments affecting this policy · material incidents at Category 1 or 2 · exception register summary · governance programme health
ISO/IEC 42001 Internal AuditAnnual (minimum)Independent internal auditorAI Governance Lead · CCOConformance assessment against ISO/IEC 42001 requirements · findings and corrective actions · filed as evidence for AI management system certification
Cross-integration: The ISO/IEC 42001 internal audit process uses the same internal audit architecture as the companion Process Quality SOP (LP-PORT-SOP-ISO_CMMI_Audit_Prep-IT_Services, Section 8). Where both audit programmes are active, a single internal audit team should coordinate to avoid duplicating effort and to ensure findings in one programme are considered in the other.
Section 13
13
Incident Reporting & Response
An incident that is not reported is a risk that is not managed. This section governs how AI incidents — and near-misses — are reported, classified, investigated, and resolved. Near-misses are treated with the same seriousness as incidents. The reporter is always protected.
13.1What Must Be Reported

The following must be reported within 24 hours of identification, using Appendix D (AI Incident Report Form), submitted to the AI Governance Lead:

Reportable Events
  • Any AI-generated output that caused harm or had the potential to cause harm to an individual, client, or the organization — including reputational harm, financial harm, and legal exposure.
  • Any unauthorized AI tool use (Shadow AI) — regardless of whether harm resulted.
  • Any AI system taking an action outside its authorized scope (for agentic AI systems, any action not within the documented authority granted under Tier 4 authorization).
  • Any data classification breach involving an AI tool — including entering Confidential or Restricted data into a tool authorized only for Internal or Public data.
  • Any AI-generated discriminatory output affecting an individual or group with a protected characteristic.
  • Any hallucination that was acted upon in a consequential decision before the error was identified.
  • Near-misses — events where harm was possible but did not occur. Near-miss reporting is mandatory, not optional. See Section 13.3.
13.2Incident Severity Classification
SeverityCriteriaResponse SLAEscalation Path
Critical Confirmed harm to an individual's rights, employment, health, credit, or safety caused or materially contributed to by AI. Confirmed data breach via an AI tool. Confirmed prohibited use under Section 8, prohibitions 1 or 2. EU AI Act reportable incident involving a GPAI model. Investigation begins within 4 hours. CEO and CCO notified within 24 hours. External reporting assessed within 48 hours. AI Governance Lead → CCO → CEO → Board AI Committee → Legal → Regulatory bodies as required by law.
Major Material data classification breach. Shadow AI use involving Confidential or Restricted data. AI-generated discriminatory output used in a consequential decision. Agentic AI action outside its authorized scope. Investigation begins within 24 hours. CCO notified within 48 hours. Corrective action plan within 10 business days. AI Governance Lead → CCO → Department Head → Legal (if personal data or regulatory implications).
Minor Shadow AI use involving Internal data. Tier requirement not followed (use proceeded without required authorization). AI output acted upon without required human review. Training obligation not completed within the required period. Investigation begins within 5 business days. Corrective action identified within 15 business days. AI Governance Lead → relevant Department Head → employee's direct manager for remediation.
Near-Miss An event where harm was possible but did not occur. Includes: AI output identified as harmful before being acted upon; Shadow AI tool used for a task involving only Public data; unauthorized data entry caught before submission; agentic AI action halted by an oversight mechanism. Reported within 24 hours. No formal investigation unless patterns emerge. Reviewed by AI Governance Lead within 10 business days. AI Governance Lead. No escalation unless the near-miss reveals a systemic gap requiring policy or control change.
13.3Near-Miss Reporting — Mandate and Protection

Near-miss reporting is mandatory under this policy. It is not a best practice — it is a governance requirement. The reason is straightforward: near-misses reveal systemic risks before they become incidents. An organization that reports and analyzes near-misses reduces its incident rate. An organization that does not, increases it.

Any person who reports a near-miss in good faith is protected from adverse consequences arising from that report. This protection is unconditional and applies regardless of the reporter's role, the sensitivity of the near-miss, or whether the near-miss involved any violation of this policy by the reporter themselves. Protection from retaliation for good-faith reporting is a condition of this policy's effectiveness — and its erosion will be treated as a governance failure reportable to the Board AI Committee.

13.4EU AI Act GPAI Incident Notification

For organizations using General-Purpose AI (GPAI) models from providers subject to the EU AI Act, the policy's incident response process must account for the following: GPAI model providers have incident notification obligations to the EU AI Office under Article 73. Where an incident involving a GPAI tool may trigger the provider's notification obligation, the AI Governance Lead must notify the provider within 24 hours and maintain records of all communications. Legal must advise on whether the organization itself has any direct reporting obligation to regulatory authorities in the specific incident context.

Cross-integration: The incident investigation and corrective action process under this section is aligned with the NCR and CAP framework in the companion Process Quality SOP (LP-PORT-SOP-ISO_CMMI_Audit_Prep-IT_Services, Sections 9 and Appendix E–F). Where both policies are active, a single corrective action process should serve both governance streams.
Section 14
14
Training & Awareness
AI literacy is a job requirement under Principle IV of this policy. This section translates that principle into a specific, tiered training programme with defined completion timelines, records obligations, and consequences for non-completion.
14.1Tier 1 — Foundation Awareness (All Staff)

Who: All employees, contractors, and interns — no exceptions. When: Within 30 days of this policy's effective date for existing staff. Within 5 days of joining for new starters. Content: What is AI in the organizational context. What this policy governs and what is expected. What tools are approved and how to find them (Appendix A). What must never be done (Section 8). How to report an incident or near-miss (Section 13, Appendix D). What Shadow AI is and why it matters. Duration: Approximately 60–90 minutes. Completion evidence: Signed acknowledgement or digital completion record. Filed with HR. Consequence of non-completion: Access to Tier 1 AI tools is suspended until training is completed. No exceptions.

14.2Tier 2 — Functional AI Literacy (AI Tool Users)

Who: All staff whose role involves regular use of AI tools listed in Appendix A at Tier 2 or above. When: Within 30 days of this policy's effective date for current AI tool users. Before first use for new tool users. Content: Data classification in the AI context. How to assess whether a use is Tier 1, 2, 3, or 4. Hallucination: how to identify it and what to do. IP and output ownership in practice (Section 10 scenarios). Data handling obligations when using AI tools. How to submit a Tier 3 or Tier 4 authorization request. Function-specific risks relevant to the individual's role. Duration: Approximately 3–4 hours over two sessions. Refresh: Annual, or within 30 days of a material policy change.

14.3Tier 3 — Governance Depth (AI Governance Lead, Department Heads, Approvers)

Who: AI Governance Lead, Chief Compliance Officer, Department Heads, anyone with authorization authority under Section 7 (Tier 3 and Tier 4), and Board AI Governance Committee members. Content: EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, and ISO/IEC 42001 — obligations relevant to the organization's AI use. Risk classification methodology and audit. Incident investigation and corrective action. KPI interpretation and governance reporting. Agentic AI governance — specific controls, authorization, and monitoring. GPAI notification obligations. Exception request assessment. Duration: Full-day programme, refreshed annually and when material regulatory changes occur.

14.4Shadow AI Awareness Module

A dedicated Shadow AI awareness module is embedded in the Tier 1 foundation training and refreshed as a standalone module for Tier 2 staff annually. The module addresses: why Shadow AI is prohibited regardless of the tool's quality; how to find and use Appendix A; the Doubt Rule (Section 3.3); and how to submit a tool for consideration for Appendix A if a useful tool is not currently approved. The module is designed to channel AI curiosity into governance, not suppress it. The organization expects its staff to find AI tools useful — the goal of this module is to make the governed path the easy path.

14.5Training Records and Access Controls

Training completion records are maintained by the AI Governance Lead in the training register. Access to AI tools at each tier is contingent on training completion for that tier — access controls are configured to enforce this. Training records are direct evidence for ISO/IEC 42001 compliance (Section 7.2 competence requirements) and are maintained for a minimum of three years from the date of completion or the date of the individual's departure, whichever is later.

Section 15
15
Exceptions Process
The exception process exists because governance that cannot accommodate legitimate edge cases will be worked around. It exists to channel legitimate innovation within governance, not to suppress it. The bar for an exception is deliberate justification — not bureaucratic compliance.
15.1What Can Be Excepted

An exception may be requested for: use of a specific AI tool not currently in Appendix A, where a legitimate business need exists and the tool cannot reasonably be substituted with an approved tool; use of an approved tool at a higher data classification than currently permitted in Appendix A, where a specific use case requires it; and deviation from specific Tier requirements in Section 7 for a defined use case, where the standard Tier process would create disproportionate operational burden relative to the risk involved.

What cannot be excepted: The seven absolute prohibitions in Section 8. The Training Data Prohibition in Section 9.4. The EU AI Act unacceptable risk prohibitions. The Agentic AI Override classification in Section 11.3. These are not policy positions subject to business judgment — they are absolute limits.

15.2Exception Request Process
StepActionResponsibilityTimeline
1Complete Appendix E (Exception Request Form) in fullRequesting employee and their Department HeadBefore submission
2Submit to AI Governance LeadDepartment HeadDay 0
3AI Governance Lead conducts initial assessment: risk classification, DPA status, legal implicationsAI Governance LeadWithin 5 business days
4CCO reviews and approves or declines. For Category 2+ exceptions, CEO co-approval required.CCO (+ CEO for Category 2+)Within 10 business days of submission
5Exception recorded in exception register. Sunset date set. Requestor notified.AI Governance LeadWithin 2 business days of approval
6Exception reviewed at sunset date. Renewed, modified, or closed.AI Governance Lead + CCOBefore sunset date
15.3Sunset Provisions

Every exception carries a sunset date — the date on which the exception automatically expires if not renewed. The maximum initial exception period is 90 days. Renewal requires the same process as the original request and a demonstration that the excepted use has been operating within the conditions of the original exception. An exception that has not been renewed by its sunset date is automatically suspended — the excepted tool or use is no longer permitted until renewal is processed and approved.

The AI Governance Lead notifies exception holders 21 days before the sunset date. The sunset date is not a courtesy deadline — it is the operational expiry of the permission. An exception operating beyond its sunset date without renewal is treated as Shadow AI use and reported as a Minor incident under Section 13.

Section 16
16
Policy Review Cycle
A governance policy that does not evolve is a governance policy that fails. The AI landscape moves faster than any annual review cycle can capture. This section governs how this policy stays current — through scheduled review, mandatory triggers, and version discipline.
16.1Annual Review

This policy is reviewed in full on an annual basis, led by the CCO and supported by the AI Governance Lead. The annual review considers: changes in the regulatory landscape, changes in the organization's AI use profile, Appendix A currency, KPI performance over the year, incident patterns, and any gaps identified through the exception process. The annual review output is a revised policy (if changes are required) or a formal confirmation that the current version remains fit for purpose, both submitted to the Board AI Governance Committee for approval.

16.2Mandatory Review Triggers
This policy must be reviewed within the specified timeframe if any of the following occurs
  • A Critical-severity AI incident (Section 13) — review Section 13 within 14 days and the full policy within 30 days of the incident being classified.
  • A material regulatory change affecting this policy's obligations under the EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 42001, GDPR, or the India DPDP Act — review affected sections within 60 days of the change taking effect.
  • A major AI model provider announces a capability, architecture, or data handling change that materially alters the risk profile of any tool in Appendix A — review Appendix A and Section 11 within 30 days of the announcement.
  • The organization enters a new market, jurisdiction, or regulated sector that changes the applicable regulatory obligations for AI use — review Section 9 and Section 3 within 60 days of operational commencement in the new context.
  • The Board AI Governance Committee determines that the organization's AI risk posture has changed materially — triggered at any quarterly Board review without waiting for the annual cycle.
  • An exception is granted that reveals a gap in Section 7 or Section 8 — review the affected section within 30 days of the exception's operational commencement.
Cross-integration: These six mandatory review triggers use the same structural pattern as the companion Process Quality SOP (Section 1.4). Both policies are governed by the same CCO function — coordinating their review cycles reduces duplication and ensures changes in one governance stream are considered in the other.
16.3Version Control

Version numbers increment by 0.1 for minor amendments (changes to Appendix A, correction of factual errors, clarification of existing provisions that do not change their substance). Version numbers increment by 1.0 for material amendments (changes to Sections 5–13, new sections, removal of sections, or changes that alter the obligations of any covered party). All amendments, regardless of type, require CCO approval and Board AI Committee notification before the revised version is published and communicated to all covered parties.

Section 17
17
Related Documents
This policy is one instrument in a larger governance ecosystem. The documents listed here govern adjacent obligations. Together with this policy, they form a governance body where AI oversight and process quality are interlocking disciplines — not parallel programs.
Document TitleTierRelationshipConnection to This Policy
Process Quality and Audit Preparation SOP
LP-PORT-SOP-ISO_CMMI_Audit_Prep-IT_Services		 Tier 2 — SOP Operational sibling The primary companion document. Governs ISO 9001:2015 and CMMI v3.0 audit preparation — the process quality framework within which AI governance operates. This policy's incident process (Section 13) is aligned with its NCR/CAP framework. The compliance monitoring (Section 12) uses the same internal audit architecture. The review cycle (Section 16) uses the same mandatory trigger structure. Both documents are operated by the same governance team and are designed to be maintained as a coherent pair, not independently.
Organizational Information Security Policy Tier 1 — Policy Foundation Establishes the organizational data classification framework that this policy references throughout Sections 7, 9, and Appendix A. The prohibition on Shadow AI (Section 8, prohibition 7) and the prohibition on using AI to probe security controls (Section 8, prohibition 6) are companion provisions to this policy's access control and acceptable use obligations.
Data Protection Policy Tier 1 — Policy Foundation Governs the organization's obligations under GDPR, the DPDP Act 2023, and other applicable data protection legislation. Section 9 of this policy is implemented within the framework established by the Data Protection Policy. The Training Data Prohibition (Section 9.4) and DPA requirements (Section 9.2) must be consistent with the Data Protection Policy's provisions on processor agreements and cross-border transfers.
Acceptable Use Policy (Technology) Tier 2 — Policy Companion Governs the use of organizational technology systems broadly. This policy's provisions on AI tools supplement and, where they conflict, supersede the Acceptable Use Policy for AI-specific uses. Shadow AI use constitutes a breach of both this policy and the Acceptable Use Policy.
Vendor and Third-Party Risk Management Policy Tier 2 — Policy Companion Governs the risk assessment and ongoing monitoring of third-party vendors. AI tool providers listed in Appendix A are subject to third-party risk management obligations under this policy. The DPA requirements (Section 9.2) and the sub-processor controls are implemented through the vendor management process.
HR Policy and Code of Conduct Tier 1 — Policy Companion Consequences for prohibited AI use (Section 8) and for non-compliance with mandatory training requirements (Section 14) are governed within the HR Policy and Code of Conduct framework. This policy defines what constitutes a breach; the HR Policy governs the consequences for employees.
Intellectual Property Policy Tier 1 — Policy Foundation Section 10 of this policy (IP and Output Ownership) operates within the framework of the IP Policy. The three ownership scenarios in Section 10.1 must be consistent with the IP Policy's general provisions on organizational IP. Legal review required where the two policies are applied to the same output.
Section 18
18
Appendices
Five working instruments. Each appendix is a governance tool — to be completed, consulted, or maintained as part of this policy's operational execution. They are not reference material. They are the policy's hands.
